Numerical Linear Algebra

Parametric Solutions and the Four Fundamental Subspaces

Parametric Solutions and the Four Fundamental Subspaces

The previous chapter introduced the column space as the first example of a subspace naturally attached to a matrix, and showed that its dimension — the rank — falls straight out of counting pivots in echelon form. This chapter finishes that story. We first show how the free variables left over after row reduction parametrize the full solution set of Ax=bAx=bAx=b, for both the homogeneous case (b=0⃗b=\vec 0b=0) and the general case. That machinery then lets us define all four fundamental subspaces of a matrix — column space, row space, null space, and left null space — and, in the second half of the chapter, uncover a striking fact about them: they come in two orthogonal pairs.

Echelon Form and Parametric Solutions

We start with the homogeneous system Ax=0⃗Ax = \vec 0Ax=0, where A∈R2×4A \in \mathbb{R}^{2\times 4}A∈R2×4 has already been reduced to echelon form.

Example: Homogeneous System
[1223∣000−3−3∣0]\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{1} & 2 & 2 & 3 & \big| & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \mathbf{-3} & -3 & \big| & 0 \end{bmatrix}[10​20​2−3​3−3​​​​00​]

The bolded entries 1\mathbf{1}1 and −3\mathbf{-3}−3 are the pivots, sitting in columns 111 and 333. Every other column — 222 and 444 — is therefore free.

Solving from the bottom row up:

x4=freex3=−x4x2=freex1=−2x2−2x3−3x4\begin{aligned} x_4 &= \text{free} \\ x_3 &= -x_4 \\ x_2 &= \text{free} \\ x_1 &= -2x_2 - 2x_3 - 3x_4 \end{aligned}x4​x3​x2​x1​​=free=−x4​=free=−2x2​−2x3​−3x4​​

Collecting terms by which free variable they multiply gives the parametric vector form of the solution set:

Nul⁡(A)=x2[−2100]+x4[−10−11].\operatorname{Nul}(A) = x_2 \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + x_4 \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.Nul(A)=x2​​−2100​​+x4​​−10−11​​.

Nothing about this process is specific to a 2×42\times 42×4 matrix — the same pattern holds for any rectangular system.

General Expression of Homogeneous Systems

Let A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n with rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A) = rrank(A)=r. Row reduction produces rrr pivots and n−rn - rn−r free variables, and the general solution to Ax=0⃗Ax = \vec 0Ax=0 can always be written as

x=xf1h1+xf2h2+⋯+xfn−rhn−r,x = x_{f_1}h_1 + x_{f_2}h_2 + \cdots + x_{f_{n-r}}h_{n-r},x=xf1​​h1​+xf2​​h2​+⋯+xfn−r​​hn−r​,

where each hih_ihi​ is a fixed vector (determined by back-substitution, exactly as above) and each xfix_{f_i}xfi​​ is a free parameter.

A non-homogeneous system Ax=bAx = bAx=b (with b≠0⃗b \ne \vec 0b=0) works the same way, with one addition: alongside the free-variable terms, a single particular solution — the specific values the basic variables take when every free variable is set to zero — gets added in.

Example: Non-Homogeneous System
[1201∣20011∣1]\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & \big| & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & \big| & 1 \end{bmatrix}[10​20​01​11​​​​21​]

The pivots sit in columns 111 and 333, so x1x_1x1​ and x3x_3x3​ are basic, and x2,x4x_2, x_4x2​,x4​ are free:

x2=freex4=freex3=1−x4x1=2−2x2−x4\begin{aligned} x_2 &= \text{free} \\ x_4 &= \text{free} \\ x_3 &= 1 - x_4 \\ x_1 &= 2 - 2x_2 - x_4 \end{aligned}x2​x4​x3​x1​​=free=free=1−x4​=2−2x2​−x4​​

which gives the parametric vector form

x=[2010]⏟particular solution+x2[−2100]+x4[−10−11].x = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{particular solution}} + x_2 \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + x_4 \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.x=particular solution​2010​​​​+x2​​−2100​​+x4​​−10−11​​.

The particular solution [2010]\begin{bmatrix}2\\0\\1\\0\end{bmatrix}​2010​​ solves Ax=bAx=bAx=b on its own (setting x2=x4=0x_2=x_4=0x2​=x4​=0); the remaining two terms are exactly the homogeneous solution from the previous example, since the coefficient matrix is identical. This is the general pattern: solution = particular solution + homogeneous solution.

The Four Fundamental Subspaces

With parametric solutions in hand, we can now name four subspaces that live naturally alongside any matrix A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n — two associated with AAA itself, and two with its transpose.

Column Space and Row Space

Column Space and Row Space
Column Space:R(A)={ Ax∣x∈Rn }⊆RmRow Space:R(AT)={ ATy∣y∈Rm }⊆Rn\begin{aligned} \text{Column Space:} \quad & R(A) = \{\, Ax \mid x \in \mathbb{R}^n \,\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m \\ \text{Row Space:} \quad & R(A^{\mathsf T}) = \{\, A^{\mathsf T}y \mid y \in \mathbb{R}^m \,\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \end{aligned}Column Space:Row Space:​R(A)={Ax∣x∈Rn}⊆RmR(AT)={ATy∣y∈Rm}⊆Rn​

Equivalently:

b∈R(A)≡Col⁡(A)  ⟺  ∃ x such that b=Ax,a∈R(AT)≡Row⁡(A)  ⟺  ∃ y such that a=ATy.\begin{aligned} b \in R(A) \equiv \operatorname{Col}(A) &\iff \exists\, x \text{ such that } b = Ax, \\ a \in R(A^{\mathsf T}) \equiv \operatorname{Row}(A) &\iff \exists\, y \text{ such that } a = A^{\mathsf T}y. \end{aligned}b∈R(A)≡Col(A)a∈R(AT)≡Row(A)​⟺∃x such that b=Ax,⟺∃y such that a=ATy.​

The row space is, quite literally, the column space of ATA^{\mathsf T}AT — every definition and fact about column spaces from the previous chapter applies here, just transposed.

Null Space and Left Null Space

The other two subspaces capture what AAA (and ATA^{\mathsf T}AT) send to zero — exactly the parametric solution sets we built above.

Null Space
Nul⁡(A)={ x∣Ax=0⃗ }⊆Rn\operatorname{Nul}(A) = \{\, x \mid Ax = \vec 0 \,\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^nNul(A)={x∣Ax=0}⊆Rn
Left Null Space
Nul⁡(AT)={ y∣ATy=0⃗ }⊆Rm\operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}) = \{\, y \mid A^{\mathsf T}y = \vec 0 \,\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^mNul(AT)={y∣ATy=0}⊆Rm

It’s called the left null space because y∈Nul⁡(AT)y \in \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T})y∈Nul(AT) is equivalent to yTA=0⃗Ty^{\mathsf T}A = \vec 0^{\mathsf T}yTA=0T — yyy annihilates AAA from the left.

Spanning Sets

Each of these four subspaces has a spanning set that falls directly out of row reduction, tying this section back to the pivot-counting arguments from the column space discussion.

Spanning Sets for the Four Fundamental Subspaces
Col⁡(A)≡R(A)  ⟶  pivot columns of ANul⁡(A)  ⟶  particular solutions of Ax=0⃗Row⁡(A)≡R(AT)  ⟶  pivot rows of the echelon form of ANul⁡(AT)  ⟶  particular solutions of ATy=0⃗\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Col}(A) \equiv R(A) &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{pivot columns of } A \\ \operatorname{Nul}(A) &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{particular solutions of } Ax = \vec 0 \\ \operatorname{Row}(A) \equiv R(A^{\mathsf T}) &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{pivot rows of the echelon form of } A \\ \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}) &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{particular solutions of } A^{\mathsf T}y = \vec 0 \end{aligned}Col(A)≡R(A)Nul(A)Row(A)≡R(AT)Nul(AT)​⟶pivot columns of A⟶particular solutions of Ax=0⟶pivot rows of the echelon form of A⟶particular solutions of ATy=0​

[!tip] Finding Nul⁡(AT)\operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T})Nul(AT) from Row Reduction Recall the permutation matrix PPP used when row-reducing AAA to echelon form, and suppose rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A) = rrank(A)=r. Split PPP into its first rrr rows and remaining m−rm-rm−r rows:

P=[P1P2],P1∈Rr×m,  P2∈R(m−r)×m.P = \begin{bmatrix} P_1 \\ P_2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad P_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{r\times m},\ \ P_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{(m-r)\times m}.P=[P1​P2​​],P1​∈Rr×m,  P2​∈R(m−r)×m.

The rows of P2P_2P2​ are exactly the combinations of the original rows of AAA that vanished during elimination — i.e., the "0=00 = 00=0" rows. Since those combinations annihilate AAA, they lie in the left null space:

Nul⁡(AT)=R(P2T).\operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}) = R(P_2^{\mathsf T}).Nul(AT)=R(P2T​).

Orthogonality of the Four Fundamental Subspaces

The four subspaces above weren’t picked arbitrarily — they come in two pairs that are not just related, but orthogonal complements of one another. To make that claim precise, we first need to define what “orthogonal complement” means for a subspace, rather than just a single vector.

Orthogonal Complement

Let VVV be a vector space and M⊆VM \subseteq VM⊆V a subspace. The orthogonal complement of MMM is

M⊥:={ x∈V∣⟨m,x⟩=0  ∀ m∈M }.M^{\perp} := \{\, x \in V \mid \langle m, x\rangle = 0 \ \ \forall\, m \in M \,\}.M⊥:={x∈V∣⟨m,x⟩=0  ∀m∈M}.
Direct Sum

The symbol ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes a direct sum. For any subspace M⊆VM \subseteq VM⊆V,

V=M⊕M⊥.V = M \oplus M^{\perp}.V=M⊕M⊥.

This means every v∈Vv \in Vv∈V can be written uniquely as

v=m+m⊥,m∈M,  m⊥∈M⊥.v = m + m^{\perp}, \qquad m \in M,\ \ m^{\perp} \in M^{\perp}.v=m+m⊥,m∈M,  m⊥∈M⊥.

With orthogonal complements defined, we can now ask the motivating question directly: is there any orthogonality relationship between the four fundamental subspaces themselves? It turns out there is, and the standard (real) inner product ⟨x,y⟩=xTy\langle x, y\rangle = x^{\mathsf T}y⟨x,y⟩=xTy is all we need to uncover it.

Orthogonality of the Four Fundamental Subspaces

For A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n,

R(A)⊥=Nul⁡(AT)andR(AT)⊥=Nul⁡(A).R(A)^{\perp} = \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}) \qquad \text{and} \qquad R(A^{\mathsf T})^{\perp} = \operatorname{Nul}(A).R(A)⊥=Nul(AT)andR(AT)⊥=Nul(A).

In words: the column space and the left null space are orthogonal complements of each other in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm, and the row space and the null space are orthogonal complements of each other in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.

Proof

Let x∈Rmx \in \mathbb{R}^mx∈Rm. By definition, x∈R(A)⊥x \in R(A)^{\perp}x∈R(A)⊥ iff ⟨x,Ay⟩=0\langle x, Ay\rangle = 0⟨x,Ay⟩=0 for every y∈Rny \in \mathbb{R}^ny∈Rn. Using the symmetry of the real inner product and the definition of the transpose:

⟨x,Ay⟩=(Ay)Tx=yTATx=⟨y,ATx⟩.\langle x, Ay \rangle = (Ay)^{\mathsf T}x = y^{\mathsf T}A^{\mathsf T}x = \langle y, A^{\mathsf T}x \rangle.⟨x,Ay⟩=(Ay)Tx=yTATx=⟨y,ATx⟩.

So x∈R(A)⊥x \in R(A)^{\perp}x∈R(A)⊥ iff ⟨y,ATx⟩=0\langle y, A^{\mathsf T}x\rangle = 0⟨y,ATx⟩=0 for every yyy, which happens iff ATx=0⃗A^{\mathsf T}x = \vec 0ATx=0 — that is, iff x∈Nul⁡(AT)x \in \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T})x∈Nul(AT). This proves R(A)⊥=Nul⁡(AT)R(A)^{\perp} = \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T})R(A)⊥=Nul(AT).

The second identity follows by applying the first result to ATA^{\mathsf T}AT in place of AAA:

R(AT)⊥=Nul⁡ ⁣((AT)T)=Nul⁡(A).■R(A^{\mathsf T})^{\perp} = \operatorname{Nul}\!\big((A^{\mathsf T})^{\mathsf T}\big) = \operatorname{Nul}(A). \qquad \blacksquareR(AT)⊥=Nul((AT)T)=Nul(A).■

Because each pair of subspaces forms an orthogonal complement, the direct sum decomposition from earlier applies immediately — splitting both Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm and Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn cleanly in two, and giving a dimension count that should look familiar from the rank–nullity theorem.

Consequences

Direct sum decompositions:

Rm=R(A)⊕Nul⁡(AT),Rn=R(AT)⊕Nul⁡(A).\mathbb{R}^m = R(A) \oplus \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}), \qquad \mathbb{R}^n = R(A^{\mathsf T}) \oplus \operatorname{Nul}(A).Rm=R(A)⊕Nul(AT),Rn=R(AT)⊕Nul(A).

Dimension counts (rank–nullity):

dim⁡R(A)+dim⁡Nul⁡(AT)=m,dim⁡R(AT)+dim⁡Nul⁡(A)=n.\dim R(A) + \dim \operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T}) = m, \qquad \dim R(A^{\mathsf T}) + \dim \operatorname{Nul}(A) = n.dimR(A)+dimNul(AT)=m,dimR(AT)+dimNul(A)=n.

Together, these four subspaces — and the orthogonality that pairs them up — form the complete geometric picture of what a matrix does: R(AT)R(A^{\mathsf T})R(AT) and Nul⁡(A)\operatorname{Nul}(A)Nul(A) partition the input space Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, while R(A)R(A)R(A) and Nul⁡(AT)\operatorname{Nul}(A^{\mathsf T})Nul(AT) partition the output space Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm. This picture will resurface directly when we reach the SVD, which builds an orthonormal basis out of exactly these four pieces.
